summary: DHH argues that how you consume media matters as much as what you consume. He describes his shift from continuous, grazing-style media consumption to a batched email-based approach using Mailbrew, which delivers throttled digests of Hacker News, Twitter, YouTube, and news sources on a schedule. This gives him the satisfying feeling of being 'done' that print newspapers once provided. He's also changed how he produces content, moving away from direct Twitter engagement toward longer-form writing on HEY World, filtered through a higher bar of 'does this warrant a post.'
key_points: Continuous, grazing-style media consumption means you're never done, which drains time and attention
Batching media into scheduled email digests recreates the print newspaper's finite, completable reading experience
Throttling Twitter to just a few curated tweets per person provides the value without the addiction
The 'how' applies to producing media too — writing longer posts with a higher bar beats reactive engagement on social platforms
Artificial constraints on consumption, even without browser blocking, can produce dramatic results when sustained as a habit
money_quotes: If you fill your mind with drivel, it'll soak your thoughts in kind. But how you choose to fill your mind matters too.
When you graze on media like this, you're never done.
Allowing you to set the budget before you reach the casino.
The days literally seem longer. In the best possible sense.
Throttling Twitter in particular feels like a personal breakthrough.
Same thoughts, principles, but filtered through the bar of 'does this warrant a post', and then shielded from the corrosive effects of likes and retweets and arguing with strangers on a stage.
Media dieting has about the same success rate for most people that actual dieting does. Which is to say abysmal.
key_insight: Switching from continuous media grazing to scheduled, throttled email digests restores the feeling of being 'done' and reclaims both time and attention.
